and leadership of an association.
I have also sought out opportunities to develop my supervisory and teaching skills. I have co-supervised Master’s
dissertations, and lectured on the Speech and Language Sciences Masters programme at UCL. These activities
are contributing towards my portfolio application for Associate Fellowship of the Higher Education Academy.
Career development: My achievements in my current role demonstrate my commitment to this research topic,
which I intend to further develop during my PhD. I am actively working with my proposed academic supervisors
and have engaged with five people living with Parkinson’s and twelve SLTs to identify research priorities for this
proposal. I therefore have an established network of experts to support this proposal. Through this fellowship I
plan to develop additional highly specialist clinical skills in the management of people living with Parkinson’s, in
particular those with complex or advanced communication and cognitive needs. I aim to be able to act as a lead
SLT at a specialist centre for Parkinson’s and related neurological disorders. I also plan to gain further research
skills, in particular in mixed-methods and clinica

Proposed training and development programme
I will use the UCL Doctoral Skills Development Programme to develop skills across the Vitae Researcher
Development Framework. I will prioritise the areas of training below to enable completion of the proposed PhD and
facilitate my development as a mixed methods researcher and practitioner academic leader.
Year 1:
Research governance: to ensure a well governed project, I will attend modules on research ethics, transparency,
data management and project management.
Literature review and library methodologies: to develop skills in critical appraisal and enable me to carry out a
scoping review (WP1)
Co-production (including Co-production Network for Wales course): PPI is key to developing complex
interventions. Through guided planning and reflection, I will build my expertise in inclusive co-production with
people with more advanced Parkinson’s.
Statistics and Research Methods (including UCL’s Centre for Applied Statistics Course): a comprehensive
introduction to research methods and statistics in year 1 will enable me to plan appropriate study design, data
collection and interpretation of statistical tests for WP3.

Description of support provided
Professor Steven Bloch has held a permanent full-time academic position at UCL since 2002. He has supervised 5
PhD students to completion and is currently supervising a further 3 PhD students (post-upgrade), with an
additional NIHR funded doctoral fellowship student to start in March 2024. The latter is a notable collaboration with
colleagues in the UCL Department of Palliative Care. Most of these PhD students have been/are experienced
healthcare professionals.

proposed project is closely aligned with his current research programme and that originally supported by his own
NIHR post-Doctoral Fellowship award (2009-2012). Professor Steven Bloch has acted as manager in my research
into Better Conversations with Parkinson’s – Telehealth. The proposed project builds on this existing collaboration.
Professor Bloch is a core member of the UCL Better Conversations Lab and works productively with the wider
research team. The proposed project will both contribute to, and benefit from

evidence.text:
Parkinson’s is a condition affecting around 120,000 people in the UK. Approximately 80% of people living with
Parkinson’s (plwP) face communication challenges. These can cause embarrassment, isolation and depression.
PlwP and their family members therefore describe needing support with conversation.
Speech and language therapy helps with speech difficulties, such as a quiet voice. However, there is limited
research on how to help plwP with other communication difficulties. These include difficulties finding words,
changed facial expressions, and problems with thinking. This is why I am developing a new therapy called Better
Conversations with Parkinson’s (BCP). BCP involves working with plwP and a family member or friend
(communication partner). Together they build skills to make long-term improvements in everyday conversations.
BCP is in the early stages of development. So far, the therapy (BCP-Telehealth) has only been delivered by a
researcher via video appointment, and to plwP with mild symptoms.
I have worked extensively with plwP and speech and language therapists (SLTs). They came up with these
research priorities:
1. Understand what plwP hope to change by completing BCP and

evidence.text:
The problem:
Parkinson’s is one of the fastest growing neurological conditions in the world, with a recorded UK prevalence of
around 22:10,000 women and 32:10,000 men (2). Approximately 70-90% of plwP experience communication
changes (3). In addition to changes to speech and voice, Parkinson’s can cause a range of communication and
cognitive difficulties, such as difficulties with pragmatics, verbal fluency and processing (4). These challenges can
affect participation in day-to-day life (5), self-esteem and quality of life (6). Furthermore, communication difficulties
can have a devastating impact on family members, relationships and care burden (7), in particular as cognitive
impairments increase (8). These issues contribute significantly to the high societal costs of Parkinson’s (9).
There is currently a lack of evidenced speech and language therapy approaches addressing the wide range of
communication and cognitive difficulties, and psychosocial impacts related to Parkinson’s. Intervention for plwP
predominantly focuses on speech impairments (10). Lee Silverman Voice Treatment (LSVT) (11), a high-intensity
programme that trains people to recalibrate vocal volume, is well evidence

any NHS Trusts are unable to routinely offer more intensive therapies (14)
and to ensure no additional costs or time expectations beyond standard care for people with communication
difficulties resulting from Parkinson’s. According to national data on SLT service delivery (22), this allows a £700
saving per person per year in comparison with more intensive therapies such as LSVT.
Review of existing evidence
Communication difficulties in Parkinson’s
Changes to speech, such as voice and articulatory impairments, are well attested and affect over 70% of plwP
(23). Parkinson’s can also affect communication in other ways, for example the production and processing of
grammar, verbal fluency, information content (4), and pragmatic and non-verbal communication (24). These
changes can cause embarrassment and reduced communicative participation (5), and affect feelings of
competence and self-esteem (6).
Standard speech and language therapy for plwP predominantly focuses on speech and impairments (10), and is
effective in the treatment of hypokinetic dysarthria (a motor speech disorder) (25). Well-evidenced treatments such
as LSVT (11) are predominantly researched amongst those with mild to m

oderate Parkinson’s (12) and suitable for
those who have sufficient cognitive ability, motivation and stimulable voice quality (13). Standard provision
therefore fails to address the needs of those who experience more severe symptoms or communication
impairments beyond speech, in particular cognitive change. SLTs report feeling able to influence voice loudness
and intelligibility, but lacking assessments and interventions to address broader language and cognitive difficulties
(10).
PlwP describe further limitations to speech and language therapy provision such as: difficulty transferring gains to
day-to-day conversations; difficulty continuing exercises at home; and that therapy does not always address
communication problems of concern (26). Suggestions for improvement include increased focus on the
psychosocial aspects of communication, and inclusion of family members in therapy. These perspectives are
shared by SLTs who report feeling less able to address issues of carryover outside of therapy (10) and describe
lacking necessary tools to target interaction or conversation with this client group (27).
Reduced effectiveness of communication can cause frustration and dissatisfaction

evidence.text:
l produce a freely available, evidence-based intervention (a refined BCP) for SLTs to use with
plwP and their communication partners, which is acceptable to plwP including those with more complex and
advanced symptoms. BCP is designed to address communication changes related to Parkinson’s, and the impact
on self-esteem, quality of life, participation, caregiver burden and health and social care costs. The long-term aim
will be a large-scale effectiveness trial of BCP for which separate funding will be sought.
I led a PPI project using consensus methods with plwP, family members and expert NHS SLTs to co-identify
outstanding areas of development for BCP (see project plan) and co-decide BCP research priorities. Based on
these priorities, the aims of the fellowship are:
1). To develop a set of outcome measures to use with BCP and for a future trial
2). To refine BCP intervention so it is acceptable to plwP with a range of communication and cognitive
impairments, and their communication partners
3). To establish the feasibility of a multi-centre, randomised controlled trial of BCP
Objectives
These aims will be achieved through 3 work packages (WPs).
WP1: outcome measurement
 Establis

ing standard care. Standard care will be co-defined by
SLTs at participating sites, and is expected to include approximately six sessions of impairment-based therapy per
participant. With PPI support, I will train SLTs to deliver BCP. Covariate adaptive randomisation will be used to
minimise imbalance of severity of Parkinson’s symptoms between the BCP and control group to control for the
possible influence of this on trial conclusions, and to allow for continuous enrolment to the study.
Recruitment: Participants will be recruited who have: a primary diagnosis of Parkinson’s with resulting
communication difficulties; an available family member or friend to take part in therapy; and adequate hearing,
vision and cognition to participate in SLT intervention. Given the high incidence of dementia in advanced
Parkinson’s (44), recruitment criteria will not exclude those with cognitive difficulties or concomitant conditions, to
address the needs of those with advanced Parkinson’s and mimic clinical reality for future implementation.
Recruitment will take place across four NHS sites (North Bristol NHS Trust, University Hospitals of Derby and
Burton NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester Universi

ty NHS Foundation Trust and Bolton NHS Foundation Trust),
with agreement in principle from SLT managers at these Trusts. Collaborating Trusts have been chosen due to
SLTs indicating sufficient resources to recruit participants and, since caseloads are more diverse, to maximise
opportunities for recruitment from under-served populations.
Sample size: This has been dealt with pragmatically based on SLT reports of caseload size, national audit data
on SLT service delivery, and expected recruitment rates based on a recent Better Conversations trial (45) and SLT
trial with plwP (46). I anticipate recruiting 40 participants over an 18-month period. Achieving a sample size of over
30 should more accurately inform a future full trial (47).
Outcomes: It is expected that outcome measures selected in WP1 might include measures of activity and
participation (Dysarthria Impact Profile (48), Communicative Participation Item Bank (49)), quality of life
(Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (50), Parkinson’s Disease Questionnaire – Carers (51), Adult Carer Quality of
Life Questionnaire (52)), personalised goals (Goal Attainment Scaling in rehabilitation (53)), and conversation

WP2: intervention refinement
 Work with PPI members (plwP, family members, expert SLTs) to refine BCP to meet the needs of plwP
with a range of communication and cognitive symptoms and severities, including adapting BCP-Telehealth
for face-to-face delivery
WP3: randomised feasibility study
Trial the refined BCP intervention with a control group (standard NHS treatment) over 4 NHS sites to ascertain for
a future full trial:
 the acceptability and adherence to an adapted BCP intervention for plwP with a range of cognitive and
communication symptoms and their family members
 a meaningful and sensitive primary outcome measure (from a set of measures decided upon in WP1) –
this will inform a sample size calculation for a future full trial
recruitment and retention rates

 the feasibility of data collection across multiple NHS sites
 costs to inform a future economic evaluation
 whether a randomised controlled trial can

be delivered as intended over multiple NHS sites
Project Plan
The WPs follow the ADAPT guidance (41) for adapting interventions to new contexts, and guidance for developing
and evaluating complex interventions (18) as a step towards implementation of BCP at scale.
WP1: Identifying key constructs plwP and SLTs want to change by completing BCP, and corresponding outcome
measures
PPI priorities: PlwP, family members and SLTs want to be able to capture meaningful changes resulting from
BCP. PPI members feel that current BCP-Telehealth outcome measures do not fully capture the potential impact
of the therapy. Outcome measures are felt to be important for clinical use alongside the intervention, and for a
future effectiveness trial.
Scoping review: I will conduct a scoping review to generate a list of relevant outcome measures reported in
previous studies. Whilst there is research into measures used clinically (27), there is no comprehensive review
identifying outcome measures for plwP which considers changes to communication impairment and the impact on
wider domains such as participation and interaction, from the perspective of plwP or communication partners. A
scoping review has been

chosen to allow identification of a broad range of outcome measures, accounting for a
diversity of relevant literature and methodologies.
Nominal group technique: Plwp, communication partners and SLTs will be recruited to three groups of 5-8
people. An open call to SLTs will be made via Clinical Excellence Networks, social media and professional
contacts. Purposive sampling will be used to include SLTs with experience of working with plwP, or experience of
CPT to learn from other fields such as aphasia. PlwP and communication partners will be recruited through
Parkinson’s charities, such as Parkinson’s UK, and through social media and flyers at support groups. For
pragmatic reasons participants will need sufficient cognition, communication and mobility to take part in face-to-
face group discussions. SLTs and communication partners will be asked to consider their own perspectives and
concepts of relevance to those with more severe symptoms unable to take part in this study. Purposive sampling
to ensure a range of communication difficulties and severities will be used in an attempt to capture a broad,
although not complete, range of perspectives. Groups will meet face to face two t

o three times. Using nominal
group technique, consensus will be reached on constructs that are most important to achieve when undergoing
BCP therapy. The study PPI group will match these constructs to existing outcome measures from the scoping
review, and identify any gaps in current outcome measures. The PPI group will select outcome measures to be
trialled in a feasibility study (WP3) and identify which constructs important to plwP and SLTs should be
incorporated into a refined BCP intervention (WP2).
WP2: Co-production of a refined BCP intervention, adapted for people with cognitive difficulties
PPI priorities: Expert SLTs in the NHS recommend adapting BCP-Telehealth for face-to-face delivery and to
ensure that it meets the needs of those with more advanced Parkinson’s. This is required to respond to the need
for evidence-based interventions for plwP with complex cognitive and communication difficulties, and to reflect the
populations SLTs work with. The co-production methods used below respond directly to areas for improvement
identified in a detailed evaluation of BCP PPI methods used to date.
Co-production: PPI members (plwP, family members, expert SLTs) will co-produce an up

dated intervention and
resources. Co-production will be informed by members’ expertise, what key stakeholders want to achieve from
BCP (WP1) and data from the BCP-Telehealth feasibility study. I will create a co-production network, with 10 core
members. A mixture of face-to-face, one-to-one or small group meetings (2-3 people), and larger group and online
meetings will be used, with a flexible approach to ensure accessibility for people with a range of needs. Members
will be trained in co-production methods to ensure shared understanding and purpose, and so that all are
empowered to fully participate. A person with Parkinson’s will be paid to act as joint co-production facilitator to
increase the strength of voice and accessibility for plwP.
Outputs: Outputs are expected to include session plans, handouts and video examples hosted on a website for
SLTs to use for free. Electronic resources will be put together with the support of UCL Educational Media Creative
Services.
Recruitment: People will be recruited from existing BCP PPI members, who have a thorough knowledge of BCP-
Telehealth research and valuable experience of living with Parkinson’s and communication difficulties. New

evidence.text:
ers (family
members or friends). A speech and language therapist (SLT) works with the person with Parkinson’s and a
communication partner to set individualised goals and build skills to improve the success of everyday
conversations. This research involves refining BCP for use with those with more complex cognitive and
communication difficulties, and preparation for a full-scale trial.
Importance for plwP: Through improved everyday conversations, BCP has the potential to improve quality of life,
communicative participation, and feelings of competence and self-esteem for plwP. Improved communication can
influence relationships and reduce isolation, with a possible impact on mental health issues that are prevalent in
Parkinson’s. The refinements proposed to BCP intend to benefit in particular an under-served group in research:
those with advanced Parkinson’s and complex communication or cognitive needs.
Importance for families: By providing therapy to communication partners alongside plwP, BCP aims to reduce
the risk of carer burden and relationship dissatisfaction (7,16) associated with communication difficulties. The
proposed refinements to BCP target carers of people with Parkinson

’s and cognitive impairments, who experience
fewer positive interactions and higher carer burden (8). By supporting carers’ communication and interaction, BCP
aligns with priorities in the NHS Long Term plan and NICE guidance (17).
Importance to stakeholders: This proposal is based on research aims co-decided by key stakeholders (plwP,
family members and SLTs). Stakeholder involvement (figure 1) is central to the proposed research which uses co-
production, a method which is recognised as essential when designing complex health and social care
interventions (18). Co-production is consistent with the principles of person-centred and personalised care upheld
within the NHS Long Term Plan. The long-term aim is to produce a freely available, evidence-based intervention
for SLTs to use with plwP and family members or friends, which is acceptable to plwP including those with more
complex and advanced symptoms.
NIHR aims: This project aligns with NIHR’s aims of bringing clinical research to under-served regions and
communities with major health needs, and of improving the lives of people with multiple long-term conditions. The
refinement of BCP is designed to meet the health needs of an u

nder-served group: plwP with more advanced
communication and cognitive symptoms, including dementias. Collaborating NHS Trusts have been chosen with
the aim of increasing recruitment from ethnic minorities and other under-served groups. Jewish and Asian
communities, and plwP with cognitive impairments will be purposively involved in the PPI network to maximise
inclusion across the research project. BCP is a holistic, individualised approach and the feasibility study inclusion
criteria are broad to align with clinical reality and support the inclusion of plwP with other long-term conditions.
Reduced NHS costs: Parkinson’s is a high-cost, high-need condition for the NHS (19), with communication
difficulties related to higher costs (20). By working towards implementation of BCP in the NHS, this research aims
to address costs associated with the impact of Parkinson’s on communication, participation and feelings of
confidence (9). Through optimising communication with carers, there is the potential to reduce resource utilisation
costs, such as hospitalisations, associated with caregiver strain in Parkinson’s (21). The intervention comprises six
sessions in recognition of the fact that m

munication difficulties to engage meaningfully with PPI; after production of the BCP intervention to engage
with potential future users and respond to feedback; and following analysis of the feasibility trial.
It is anticipated that the refined BCP intervention will be a valuable resource for SLTs to use with plwP, in
particular for those with cognitive difficulties and advanced symptoms. By improving day-to-day conversations,
BCP can likely improve quality of life for plwP, communicative participation, and feelings of competence and self-
esteem. Directly working with family members to address communication challenges may also reduce caregiver
burden and third party disability.
BCP has potential to reduce NHS costs associated with this high-cost, high-need condition, and save money
compared to more intensive therapies such as LSVT. Through improving everyday conversations between plwP
and carers, there is the possibility of reducing money spent on the effect of Parkinson’s on mood, communication
and confidence, and reducing costs associated with caregiver strain. Feasibility trial data will provide estimates of
delivery costs, and an appropriate health related quality of life meas

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
PPI is central to BCP research (figure 1), which to date has been conducted in collaboration with key stakeholders
(plwP, family members, expert SLTs). I have facilitated steering and co-production groups responsible for:
overseeing the management of the project, informing recruitment and ethical procedures, generating a plan for
dissemination and future research and co-producing BCP-Telehealth therapy. Co-production involved equal power
in decision making between plwP, family members, SLTs and researchers, including decisions on: written and
video resources; therapy aims, structure and activities; and processes such as goal setting and monitoring
change.
I successfully secured a UCL Public Engagement grant to identify research priorities for this proposal. This
involved:
 advisory groups meeting online to identify initial research priorities
 use of social media to gather broad perspectives from plwP, researchers and SLTs on research priorities
 meeting with expert SLTs from five NHS Trusts, including those who work with people from a range of
ethnic origins and SLTs

ely seeking people with more severe communication and
cognitive deficits to improve inclusion of under-served groups.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
PlwP, family members or friends and expert SLTs will be involved as equal partners throughout the co-design, co-
production and co-evaluation of this project (figure 1). I have expertise in co-production and an established PPI
network, with members who would like to continue working on the BCP project. I will collaborate with a PPI
network of 10 people to:
 co-design the research process (e.g. recruitment procedures and information for potential participants)
 co-investigate and decide upon outcome measures (WP1)
 update the BCP intervention (WP2)
 co-evaluate findings from WP3
 support dissemination (e.g. writing accessible summaries and jointly presenting findings)
I have used the NIHR Research Design Service’s Equality Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) toolkit to reflect on how to
improve EDI in this research. PPI forms a key part of aiming to minimise inequalities in outcomes, experience

the aim of increasing recruitment from ethnic minorities and other under-served groups. Jewish and Asian
communities, and plwP with cognitive impairments will be purposively involved in the PPI network to maximise
inclusion across the research project. BCP is a holistic, individualised approach and the feasibility study inclusion
criteria are broad to align with clinical reality and support the inclusion of plwP with other long-term conditions.
Reduced NHS costs: Parkinson’s is a high-cost, high-need condition for the NHS (19), with communication
difficulties related to higher costs (20). By working towards implementation of BCP in the NHS, this research aims
to address costs associated with the impact of Parkinson’s on communication, participation and feelings of
confidence (9). Through optimising communication with carers, there is the potential to reduce resource utilisation
costs, such as hospitalisations, associated with caregiver strain in Parkinson’s (21). The intervention comprises six
sessions in recognition of the fact that m

nsufficient recruitment will be mitigated through PPI maximising accessibility of
the study, and through close liaison with NHS sites. Face-to-face delivery is being investigated following
feedback that telehealth is a barrier to BCP-Telehealth recruitment. I will use the SEAR framework (58) at
each recruiting site to understand the recruitment process and improve it through additional support to
recruiting SLTs as required. Recruitment and retention data will be collected to predict rates for a future
trial.
3. Inclusion of under-served populations: Current Parkinson’s research largely concerns a cohort of
participants that does not resemble the diversity of plwP (43). I have used the NIHR Research Design
Service’s Equality Diversity and Inclusion toolkit to address this issue. For WP3, I aim to recruit over 10%
from minority ethnic groups to more closely reflect the UK population, at least 5% with complex
Parkinson’s and 10% with a cognitive impairment, given people with complex Parkinson’s make up a
significant number of speech and language therapy referrals (10) and to reflect the high incidence of
cognitive difficulties (59). Collaborating Trusts’ caseloads include under-serve

d groups. I will seek advice

develop strategies to increase diversity in participant recruitment, and mentoring from Dr Victoria

research involving under-served populations, in particular where informed consent can be challenging.

Proposed training and development programme
I will use the UCL Doctoral Skills Development Programme to develop skills across the Vitae Researcher
Development Framework. I will prioritise the areas of training below to enable completion of the proposed PhD and
facilitate my development as a mixed methods researcher and practitioner academic leader.
Year 1:
Research governance: to ensure a well governed project, I will attend modules on research ethics, transparency,
data management and project management.
Literature review and library methodologies: to develop skills in critical appraisal and enable me to carry out a
scoping review (WP1)
Co-production (including Co-production Network for Wales course): PPI is key to developing complex
interventions. Through guided planning and reflection, I will build my expertise in inclusive co-production with
people with more advanced Parkinson’s.
Statistics and Research Methods (including UCL’s Centre for Applied Statistics Course): a comprehensive
introduction to research methods and statistics in year 1 will enable me to plan appropriate study design, data
collection and interpretation of statistical tests for WP3.

The host Department is an international centre of excellence in speech, language and communication disorders
research. Staff hold awards from a range of funders including NIHR, MRC, ESRC and third sector funders such as
the Alzheimer’s Society and Parkinson’s UK. The department currently hosts four pre-doctoral clinical and
practitioner academic fellowship (PCAF) awardees, one pre-doctoral local authority fellowship awardee, and
another 16 completed PCAF awardees. Two current students are NIHR doctoral funded. Three NIHR funded
students have recently completed PhDs, with one awarded a Development and Skills Enhancement Award (Oct
2020-Dec 2021), and more recently an Advanced Fellowship (July 2022-June 2027).
Philippa will join a vibrant community of doctoral and post-doctoral researchers. Our doctoral community currently

Professor Steven Bloch has held a permanent full-time academic position at UCL since 2002. He has supervised 5

healthcare professionals.
With a clinical focus on conversation-based interventions for people with progressive neurological diseases, the
proposed project is closely aligned with his current research programme and that originally supported by his own
NIHR post-Doctoral Fellowship award (2009-2012). Professor Steven Bloch has acted as manager in my research
into Better Conversations with Parkinson’s – Telehealth. The proposed project builds on this existing collaboration.
Professor Bloch is a core member of the UCL Better Conversations Lab and works productively with the wider
research team. The proposed project will both contribute to, and benefit from

and the MNDA.

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
PPI is central to BCP research (figure 1), which to date has been conducted in collaboration with key stakeholders
(plwP, family members, expert SLTs). I have facilitated steering and co-production groups responsible for:
overseeing the management of the project, informing recruitment and ethical procedures, generating a plan for
dissemination and future research and co-producing BCP-Telehealth therapy. Co-production involved equal power
in decision making between plwP, family members, SLTs and researchers, including decisions on: written and
video resources; therapy aims, structure and activities; and processes such as goal setting and monitoring
change.
I successfully secured a UCL Public Engagement grant to identify research priorities for this proposal. This
involved:
 advisory groups meeting online to identify initial research priorities
 use of social media to gather broad perspectives from plwP, researchers and SLTs on research priorities
 meeting with expert SLTs from five NHS Trusts, including those who work with people from a range of
ethnic origins and SLTs

at a Parkinson’s Centre of Excellence, to explore current barriers to implementing
BCP and what our next steps in research should be
 working with an engagement group of plwP to synthesise this information and use consensus methods to
identify key research questions
The research aims, objectives and proposal for this study arose directly from the research priorities identified by
the engagement group. Group members have also co-authored the plain English summary of this application.
I have co-authored a paper evaluating BCP PPI processes based on survey responses from PPI group members,
documentation, outputs, and reflection using the Public Involvement Impact Assessment Framework. This
evaluation has enabled me to identify areas for development which are implemented in this proposal, for example:
having one core PPI group to allow for a stronger PPI voice; accounting for sufficient time and funding to explore
issues in depth; running face-to-face and online PPI meetings with different group sizes depending on participant
need; visiting support groups (e.g. The Jewish Care Disability Support and Social group, and Hema’s café) for
people from different ethnic origins; and purposiv

munication difficulties to engage meaningfully with PPI; after production of the BCP intervention to engage

It is anticipated that the refined BCP intervention will be a valuable resource for SLTs to use with plwP, in
particular for those with cognitive difficulties and advanced symptoms. By improving day-to-day conversations,
BCP can likely improve quality of life for plwP, communicative participation, and feelings of competence and self-
esteem. Directly working with family members to address communication challenges may also reduce caregiver
burden and third party disability.
BCP has potential to reduce NHS costs associated with this high-cost, high-need condition, and save money
compared to more intensive therapies such as LSVT. Through improving everyday conversations between plwP
and carers, there is the possibility of reducing money spent on the effect of Parkinson’s on mood, communication
and confidence, and reducing costs associated with caregiver strain. Feasibility trial data will provide estimates of

meetings will be used, with a flexible approach to ensure accessibility for people with a range of needs. Members
will be trained in co-production methods to ensure shared understanding and purpose, and so that all are
empowered to fully participate. A person with Parkinson’s will be paid to act as joint co-production facilitator to
increase the strength of voice and accessibility for plwP.
Outputs: Outputs are expected to include session plans, handouts and video examples hosted on a website for
SLTs to use for free. Electronic resources will be put together with the support of UCL Educational Media Creative
Services.
Recruitment: People will be recruited from existing BCP PPI members, who have a thorough knowledge of BCP-
Telehealth research and valuable experience of living with Parkinson’s and communication difficulties. New
m

embers will also be recruited to challenge thinking and bring different perspectives. I have significant experience
of supporting complex communication needs, and will selectively recruit plwP with cognitive difficulties from
rehabilitation clinics. I will also build collaborative relationships and recruit from minority ethnic groups to include
people previously less consulted in Parkinson’s research (42,43). For example, I will attend Hema’s café, a
network for plwP in the Asian community, and the Jewish Care Disability Support and Social group, given the
increased incidence of Parkinson’s amongst people of Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry.
WP3: Feasibility study of refined BCP
PPI priorities: PPI members emphasise the importance of a future full-scale trial of BCP to demonstrate
effectiveness. Given different models of care nationwide, stakeholders feel it is important to trial BCP in different
NHS settings.
Feasibility phase of intervention development: This aims to evaluate the research design and BCP itself prior
to a future full-scale trial (18). This multi-centre randomised feasibility study will compare a treatment group
receiving adapted BCP intervention to a control group receiv

ely seeking people with more severe communication and
cognitive deficits to improve inclusion of under-served groups.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
PlwP, family members or friends and expert SLTs will be involved as equal partners throughout the co-design, co-
production and co-evaluation of this project (figure 1). I have expertise in co-production and an established PPI
network, with members who would like to continue working on the BCP project. I will collaborate with a PPI
network of 10 people to:
 co-design the research process (e.g. recruitment procedures and information for potential participants)
 co-investigate and decide upon outcome measures (WP1)
 update the BCP intervention (WP2)
 co-evaluate findings from WP3
 support dissemination (e.g. writing accessible summaries and jointly presenting findings)
I have used the NIHR Research Design Service’s Equality Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) toolkit to reflect on how to
improve EDI in this research. PPI forms a key part of aiming to minimise inequalities in outcomes, experience

and
access as per the NHS 2022/2023 priorities. Based on feedback from previous PPI work and evaluation, I plan to:
recruit PPI members from support groups targeted at plwP from under-served groups

 collaborate with Parkinson’s UK Race Equality in Research Steering group to include people from cultural
backgrounds who have been previously less consulted in Parkinson’s research
 selectively recruit from rehabilitation centres to include PPI members with more severe communication
and cognitive difficulties
 use a flexible approach, with specific methods guided by the needs of participants to ensure that people
with diverse needs are able to meaningfully engage. For example, I will travel for one-to-one face-to-face
interviews to include plwP with more advanced symptoms, hold small (2-3 people) and large group
meetings, and offer online and in-person meetings.
All PPI members will be compensated at rates recommended by NIHR guidance. I have budgeted for 4-6 meetings
per year to allow significant time for PPI involvement, and someone with Parkinson’s will act as a joint facilitator.

ge 1 and co-producing work-package 2. Co-production is
recognised as essential when designing interventions for health and social care services in frameworks and
guidance for developing complex interventions. PPI members will be reimbursed at rates recommended by NIHR
guidance (https://www.nihr.ac.uk/documents/payment-guidance-for-researchers-and-professionals/27392). I have
budgeted for 10 participants (plwP or family members or friends) to participate for the equivalent of bi-monthly 2-
hour meetings, or half a day quarterly over the course of the study (£50 x 30 meetings x 10 = £15,000). I have
followed feedback from current PPI group members in allocating more time to PPI to allow time for full exploration
of ideas and to strengthen the voice of stakeholders. Extensive time for meaningful involvement is required given
the time taken to make topics accessible to people with complex communication and cognitive needs. Work
package 2 will involve more regular meetings (4 additional half day meetings) to co-produce the BCP intervention.
Investment in stakeholders’ time is key to ensure that the intervention meets the needs of plwP, family members
and SLTs, therefore maximising accep

tability and the likelihood of successful future implementation in the NHS.
The cost of this additional time is £3000.
Year 1 Year 2 Year 3 Year 4 Year 5 Total Cost (£)
3000 6000 3000 3000 3000 18000
Description: PPI facilitator fees and training
Type of cost: HEI
Please describe fully: PPI facilitator fees and training
Justification of Cost: I have responded to current stakeholder feedback, who have indicated that the strength of
voice of people living with Parkinson’s could be increased by having someone with Parkinson’s who acts as a joint
facilitator with the researcher at PPI meetings. It is anticipated that this will require an additional half day of their
time per meeting in order to prepare. Reimbursement rates are as per NIHR guidance
(https://www.nihr.ac.uk/documents/payment-guidance-for-researchers-and-professionals/27392) and add up to
£2,550 in total. Co-production Network for Wales provide a 12-week course (£198) to support structured reflection
on co-production and supports the planning of a co-production approach. This training is required for the facilitator
of the meetings given the complexity of accommodating the communication and cognitive needs of PPI members,

and co-producing using different methods (group and one-to-one meetings). The facilitator will attend the course
with the lead applicant.
Year 1 Year 2 Year 3 Year 4 Year 5 Total Cost (£)
648 750 450 450 450 2748
Description: PPI training
Type of cost: HEI
Please describe fully: PPI training
Justification of Cost: The PPI approach in this project is co-production, which involves equal power and decision
making between all members of the co-production group. To ensure a shared group understanding of co-
production principles, I plan to offer introductory training in co-production to all ten PPI members. Co-production
Network for Wales offer a self-directed 2-hour course (£30) into the fundamental ideas and models that underpin
co-production. This would provide a basis for the group to then discuss together, to ensure all participants feel
empowered to meaningfully contribute.

300 0 0 0 0 300
Description: Travel to individual PPI members for face-to-face meetings
Type of cost: HEI
Please describe fully: Travel to individual PPI members for face-to-face meetings
Justification of Cost: Feedback from current PPI members is that meeting o

nline is a barrier to people with more
advanced Parkinson’s. PPI members have therefore suggested offering face to face visits in people’s homes to
minimise the effect of fatigue, mobility or cognitive issues. This is not expected to be required for all PPI members.
It is important to ensure inclusion of under-served groups in this research project. I have therefore budgeted for bi-
annual visits to three four participants, using a day travel card to London zones 1-6 (£191.40) as an estimated
travel cost. It is as yet unknown where PPI members will live; PPI participants will be recruited nationally in order
to maximise involvement of under-served groups. Where possible travel will be combined to minimise costs. This
therefore represents a maximum cost.

1148 1148 1148 1148 1148 5740
Description: PPI travel to meetings in London
Type of cost: HEI
Please describe fully: PPI travel to meetings in London
Justification of Cost: As based on advice from people living with Parkinson’s, I will offer a mixture of face-to-face
and online meetings to co-production group members to ensure maximum accessibility. I plan for 2 meetings per
year to

be held face-to-face, or for a smaller number of participants to meet face to face more frequently,
dependent on participant need. Travel costs are unknown given that participants will be recruited nationally.
Estimated journey costs have been calculated based on a standard day return 1 hour from London (£77.30). This
amounts to £7,730 for 4 meetings / year for 5 participants or 2 meetings / year for 10 participants.

evidence.text:
l produce a freely available, evidence-based intervention (a refined BCP) for SLTs to use with
plwP and their communication partners, which is acceptable to plwP including those with more complex and
advanced symptoms. BCP is designed to address communication changes related to Parkinson’s, and the impact
on self-esteem, quality of life, participation, caregiver burden and health and social care costs. The long-term aim
will be a large-scale effectiveness trial of BCP for which separate funding will be sought.
I led a PPI project using consensus methods with plwP, family members and expert NHS SLTs to co-identify
outstanding areas of development for BCP (see project plan) and co-decide BCP research priorities. Based on
these priorities, the aims of the fellowship are:
1). To develop a set of outcome measures to use with BCP and for a future trial
2). To refine BCP intervention so it is acceptable to plwP with a range of communication and cognitive
impairments, and their communication partners
3). To establish the feasibility of a multi-centre, randomised controlled trial of BCP
Objectives
These aims will be achieved through 3 work packages (WPs).
WP1: outcome measurement
 Establis

h what plwP, family members and SLTs hope to achieve by completing BCP therapy
 Identify suitably sensitive and robust therapy outcome measures that measure changes important to plwP,
family members and SLTs, and that align with the expected impacts of BCP therapy
WP2: intervention refinement
 Work with PPI members (plwP, family members, expert SLTs) to refine BCP to meet the needs of plwP
with a range of communication and cognitive symptoms and severities, including adapting BCP-Telehealth
for face-to-face delivery
WP3: randomised feasibility study
Trial the refined BCP intervention with a control group (standard NHS treatment) over 4 NHS sites to ascertain for
a future full trial:
 the acceptability and adherence to an adapted BCP intervention for plwP with a range of cognitive and
communication symptoms and their family members
 a meaningful and sensitive primary outcome measure (from a set of measures decided upon in WP1) –
this will inform a sample size calculation for a future full trial
recruitment and retention rates

 the feasibility of data collection across multiple NHS sites
 costs to inform a future economic evaluation
